original_hypothesis.title: RewardsDistributor claim_many Missing AVM Owner Resolution Causes Rebase Loss
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: RewardsDistributor.claim_many() does not resolve AVM original owners for expired locks. For expired non-permanent locks, it sends rewards directly to ownerOf(tokenId) which is the AutoVotingEscrow contract. The single claim() function correctly checks `avm.tokenIdToAVMId(_tokenId) != 0` and resolves via getOriginalOwner(), but claim_many completely omits this logic. Rebase rewards for AVM-managed expired locks are sent to AVE contracts with no recovery.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: claim() and claim_many() should produce identical behavior, but claim_many lacks the AVM routing logic present in claim(). Anyone can call claim_many for any token, routing AVM rewards incorrectly.

proof_of_signal.pattern: claim_many: `address _nftOwner = IVotingEscrow(_voting_escrow).ownerOf(_tokenId)` without AVM check; contrast with claim() lines 130-140 which checks avm.tokenIdToAVMId

preconditions: AVM-managed veNFT has an expired lock (non-permanent, non-smNFT)
Rebase rewards have accumulated for the tokenId
claim_many is called (by anyone) with the AVM-managed tokenId

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: claim_many sends to ownerOf (AVE contract); claim sends to avm.getOriginalOwner (user). Different routing for same token.
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: No admin function to redirect or recover misrouted rebases
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time an AVM-managed lock expires and claim_many is called

operational_questions: Are there AVM-managed tokens with expired non-permanent locks?
Has claim_many been called for AVM-managed tokens?
Is there an automated process that calls claim_many?
validation_requirements: Compare claim() vs claim_many() output for same AVM-managed expired tokenId
Verify AVE contract has no token recovery function

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: User enables auto-voting with a non-permanent lock
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Anyone calls claim_many([tokenId]) — rewards sent to AVE contract
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None — anyone can call claim_many for any tokenId

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Rebase rewards permanently lost for affected AVM users
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: AVM users with expiring locks lose rebase income
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Rebase distribution for AVM-managed expired locks

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: BLACK tokens accumulating in AVE contracts from claim_many calls
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Mismatched rebase distributions between claim and claim_many paths

validation_result.mechanism: missing AVM owner resolution in claim_many
validation_result.impact_summary: claim_many() omits the AVM original-owner check present in claim(), sending rebase rewards for expired AVM-managed locks to the AutoVotingEscrow contract (which has no token recovery), permanently locking those funds.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The RewardsDistributor contract has two functions for claiming rebase rewards: claim() and claim_many(). Both handle expired non-permanent locks by transferring tokens directly to the NFT owner (rather than compounding via deposit_for). However, claim() includes an additional check for AVM-managed tokens that resolves the original depositor via avm.getOriginalOwner(), while claim_many() simply uses IVotingEscrow.ownerOf() which returns the AutoVotingEscrow contract address for AVM-managed tokens. The AutoVotingEscrow contract has no ERC20 withdrawal or recovery function, meaning any tokens sent there are permanently locked.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: Direct comparison of claim() and claim_many() in RewardsDistributor.sol shows the defect clearly. claim() has lines checking `if (address(avm) != address(0) && avm.tokenIdToAVMId(_tokenId) != 0) { _nftOwner = avm.getOriginalOwner(_tokenId); }` while claim_many() has only `address _nftOwner = IVotingEscrow(_voting_escrow).ownerOf(_tokenId);` with no AVM resolution. AutoVotingEscrow.sol contains no function to withdraw arbitrary ERC20 tokens.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: Missing AVM owner resolution. After resolving _nftOwner via ownerOf(), claim() checks avm.tokenIdToAVMId() and overwrites _nftOwner with avm.getOriginalOwner(). claim_many() skips this entirely, sending tokens to the AutoVotingEscrow contract address.
This is the correct implementation showing the AVM resolution logic that is missing from claim_many(). Lines with avm.tokenIdToAVMId and avm.getOriginalOwner show the intended behavior.

calling_sequence: 1. Attacker or anyone calls claim_many([tokenId]) where tokenId is AVM-managed with expired lock
2. _claim() computes rebase rewards for the tokenId
4. ownerOf(tokenId) returns the AutoVotingEscrow contract address (not the original user)
5. IERC20(token).transfer(AutoVotingEscrowAddress, amount) sends rewards to AVE contract
6. AutoVotingEscrow has no ERC20 withdrawal function - tokens are permanently locked
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: claim_many() -> for loop -> _claim() returns amount > 0 -> locked().end < block.timestamp && !isPermanent -> ownerOf() returns AutoVotingEscrow address -> transfer() sends tokens to AVE contract with no recovery mechanism. The claim() function has the same flow but adds an AVM check: `if (address(avm) != address(0) && avm.tokenIdToAVMId(_tokenId) != 0) { _nftOwner = avm.getOriginalOwner(_tokenId); }` which correctly resolves to the original depositor via AutoVotingEscrow.lockOwner().

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts provided for RewardsDistributor configuration. The defect is inherent in the contract code regardless of deployment configuration.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack scenario is valid and realistic: (1) A user enables auto-voting for a non-permanent lock via enableAutoVoting(). The NFT is transferred to an AutoVotingEscrow instance. (2) The lock expires naturally (end < block.timestamp). (3) Anyone calls claim_many([tokenId]) - this is permissionless. (4) The reward amount is computed correctly via _claim(), but sent to the AutoVotingEscrow contract instead of the original owner. (5) The AutoVotingEscrow contract (AutoVotingEscrow.sol) has only: acceptLock, releaseLock, lockOwner, isFull, getLockCount, voteLocks, resetLocks, getTotalVotingPower, getLocks - none of which withdraw ERC20 tokens. The tokens are permanently lost. Note: even the AutoVotingEscrow.voteLocks() function calls RewardsDistributor.claim() (the single-token version) which DOES have the AVM check, further confirming the inconsistency is unintentional.
validation_result.impact_assessment: HIGH severity. Any AVM-managed veNFT with a non-permanent, non-smNFT lock that expires while in the AVM will have its rebase rewards permanently lost if claim_many is called instead of claim. Since claim_many is permissionless and commonly used for batch operations, this is highly likely to occur. The AutoVotingEscrow contract has no token recovery mechanism, and no admin function exists to redirect the lost tokens. A contract upgrade would be needed to recover funds.
recommended_mitigations: Add AVM owner resolution to claim_many() identical to claim(): after `address _nftOwner = IVotingEscrow(_voting_escrow).ownerOf(_tokenId);`, add `if (address(avm) != address(0) && avm.tokenIdToAVMId(_tokenId) != 0) { _nftOwner = avm.getOriginalOwner(_tokenId); }`
Refactor reward routing into a shared internal function called by both claim() and claim_many() to prevent future divergence
Add an ERC20 recovery function to AutoVotingEscrow as a safety net for accidentally received tokens
Consider preventing non-permanent locks from being added to AVM, or auto-extending locks that are about to expire
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy VotingEscrow, RewardsDistributor, AutoVotingEscrowManager, and AutoVotingEscrow. 2. Create a non-permanent lock with create_lock(). 3. Enable auto-voting via enableAutoVoting(). 4. Advance time past lock expiry. 5. Deposit rebase tokens and checkpoint. 6. Call claim_many([tokenId]) and observe tokens sent to AutoVotingEscrow address. 7. Verify no function on AutoVotingEscrow can recover the tokens. 8. Compare with claim(tokenId) which correctly routes to original owner.
uncertainties: Whether any off-chain system or bot preferentially calls claim() vs claim_many() for AVM-managed tokens
Whether in practice non-permanent locks are used with AVM (the protocol may discourage it off-chain)
additional_testing_needed: Verify that AutoVotingEscrow truly has no ERC20 recovery path (confirmed by code review but should be tested)
Test whether the AutoVotingEscrow.voteLocks() path (which calls claim(), not claim_many()) is the only automated claim path
Check if any existing AVM-managed tokens have expired non-permanent locks on the live deployment
